summary: In his 1996 essay, Bill Gates predicts that content will be the primary driver of profitability on the Internet, mirroring the evolution of broadcasting. He emphasizes that successful digital content requires interactivity and depth rather than simply porting print media online, and foresees micro-transactions as the key to monetizing digital publishing.

principles.statement: Content is where the real money will be made on the Internet.
principles.rationale: Just as in the television revolution, the long-term winners of the Internet will be those who use the medium to deliver information and entertainment, rather than those who just manufacture the hardware.
principles.source_quote: Content is where I expect much of the real money will be made on the Internet, just as it was in broadcasting.

principles.statement: Content providers must be paid for the Internet to thrive.
principles.rationale: For the ecosystem to grow into a marketplace of ideas and experiences, creators need compensation. While advertising and subscriptions may struggle initially, micro-transactions will eventually liberate publishers to charge small amounts.
principles.source_quote: For the Internet to thrive, content providers must be paid for their work.

principles.statement: Interactive advertising requires a different approach than traditional media.
principles.rationale: Because users can click for more depth, the initial advertisement does not need to carry all the information; it only needs to capture the user's attention.
principles.source_quote: An advantage of interactive advertising is that an initial message needs only to attract attention rather than convey much information.

mental_models.name: The Multimedia Photocopier
mental_models.description: Viewing the Internet as a frictionless duplication machine that allows material to be copied and distributed worldwide at basically zero marginal cost to the publisher.
mental_models.example: Individual creators with just a PC and a modem can publish content to a global audience, and researchers can bypass expensive, slow printed journals to publish scientific findings directly.

mental_models.name: The Broadcasting Analogy
mental_models.description: Using the historical evolution of television to predict the economic future of the Internet. Hardware creates the initial industry, but content delivery captures the long-term value.
mental_models.example: Comparing the manufacturing of TV sets to the creation of PCs/networks, while equating TV networks to future interactive digital content providers.

heuristics: No company is too small to participate in supplying information or entertainment on the Internet.
In interactive advertising, the initial message needs only to attract attention, not convey all the information.

signature_quotes.text: Content is where I expect much of the real money will be made on the Internet, just as it was in broadcasting.
In a sense, the Internet is the multimedia equivalent of the photocopier.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the low cost of duplication and distribution on the web.

signature_quotes.text: If people are to be expected to put up with turning on a computer to read a screen, they must be rewarded with deep and extremely up-to-date information that they can explore at will.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the requirements for successful online magazines.

signature_quotes.text: Those who succeed will propel the Internet forward as a marketplace of ideas, experiences, and products-a marketplace of content.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding thought on the future of the Internet.

anti_patterns.description: Taking what a magazine has in print and simply moving it to the electronic realm.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: There isn't enough depth or interactivity in print content to overcome the drawbacks of reading on a screen. Users require audio, video, and personal involvement.

anti_patterns.description: Charging high subscription rates for an electronic community.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: As soon as a site charges a subscription, the number of visitors drops dramatically, which destroys the value proposition for advertisers.

anti_patterns.description: Using big images in online advertisements.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They take a long time to download, annoying users who are accustomed to rapidly flipping printed pages.
